title: Iterators
description: Beyond the basics from STL Iterators: iterator categories per container, const_iterator, reverse iteration, and the real danger of invalidation.
objectives: Know which iterator category each container we've covered provides
Understand iterator invalidation and why it matters

sections.text: 1. Recap: The Basics from STL Iterators
STL Iterators covered begin(), end(), *it, and it++. Now we go a level deeper into how iterators actually differ between containers, and a genuine danger worth knowing about.
2. Not All Iterators Are Equally Powerful

rows: Move forward only, one step at a time

sections.text: This directly explains forward_list's forward-only iterators from its own topic, and why vector's iterators can do things (like it + 5, jumping five positions at once) that a list iterator cannot — the underlying memory layout determines what's actually possible.
A const_iterator can read elements but cannot be used to modify them, providing a compile-time guarantee that a traversal won't accidentally change the container.

rbegin() and rend() give a reverse_iterator that walks the container back to front — rbegin() effectively starts at the last element, rend() marks one position before the first. The rest of the pattern (!=, ++, *) stays exactly the same as forward iteration.

This connects directly to Delete's dangling pointer warning: if push_back() triggers vector's internal resize, the old memory block is freed and a new, bigger one is used instead — any iterator obtained before that resize now points at memory that's no longer valid. Modifying a container can invalidate iterators that were pointing into it; always be cautious about using an iterator after a container has been changed.

sections.title: A bookmark in a book someone rebinds
sections.text: Recall the universal bookmark analogy from STL Iterators. If someone completely rebinds the book while your bookmark is still in it — removing pages, adding new ones, changing the whole physical structure — your bookmark's old page number might no longer correspond to anything sensible. That's iterator invalidation: the container changed shape underneath the iterator that was pointing into it.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using an iterator after the container it points into has been resized or modified — it may be invalidated, leading to undefined behavior.
Assuming every container's iterator supports jumping ahead with it + 5 — only random-access iterators (vector, deque, array) support that; list/set/map iterators only support ++/--.
Trying to modify a container's element through a const_iterator — it's specifically designed to prevent that.
Mixing up rbegin()/rend() direction — rbegin() is the LAST element, rend() is one before the FIRST, the reverse of begin()/end().

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What's the difference between a forward, bidirectional, and random-access iterator?
questions.answer: Forward iterators only move ahead one step at a time; bidirectional iterators can also move backward; random-access iterators can additionally jump directly to any position, like it + 5.

questions.question: What does const_iterator guarantee?
questions.answer: That the container's elements can be read through it, but not modified — a compile-time safeguard against accidental changes during traversal.

questions.question: What is iterator invalidation?
questions.answer: When a container is modified in a way that makes previously obtained iterators into it no longer valid — for example, a vector resizing during push_back(), leaving old iterators pointing at freed memory.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Iterators come in categories with different capabilities: forward-only (forward_list), bidirectional (list, set, map), and random-access (vector, deque, array), which also supports direct jumps like it + 5. const_iterator allows reading but not modifying through it, and rbegin()/rend() provide reverse traversal, walking from the last element back to the first. A genuinely important danger is iterator invalidation: modifying a container, such as triggering a vector resize during push_back(), can leave previously obtained iterators pointing at freed memory, exactly the dangling-pointer problem from delete applied to iterators.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Iterators
sections.text: Forward < Bidirectional < Random-access, in capability. const_iterator = look, don't touch. rbegin()/rend() = walk it backward. Modify the container, and old iterators might dangle.
